entity_description: American supermarket chain

explicit_text: My birth father was deported to Japan when World War II started. My mother, alone with three kids, placed me in the Manzanar Children's Village. It was a difficult time during World War II, and she thought it was best for me. After the war, my family reunited.

entity_description: World War II internment camp for Japanese-Americans
explicit_text: My family was held at the Rohwer War Relocation Center in Arkansas during the war. As a child, I didn't grasp the fear my parents felt. explicit_text: In 1941, I was stationed in the Philippines when the war broke out. Our commander, Boyd "Buzz" Wagner, was a real hero, one of the first aces in World War II. He was blinded in a dogfight over Manila, but managed to land his plane. It was a terrifying time.
implicit_text: In 1941, I was stationed in the Philippines when the conflict began. Our commander, Boyd "Buzz" Wagner, was a real hero, one of the first aces of that global fight. He was blinded in a dogfight over Manila, but managed to land his plane. It was a terrifying time.

entity_description: consolidated city and county in California, United States
explicit_text: In 1940, headed to Manila, we boarded trains. Shades were drawn from Detroit all the way to San Francisco. The military didn't want anyone knowing we were moving troops. We stretched our legs in Reno, then onward.
implicit_text: In 1940, headed to Manila, we boarded trains. Shades were drawn from Detroit all the way to the coast. The military didn't want anyone knowing we were moving troops. We stretched our legs in Reno, then onward.

entity_description: census-designated place in the U.S. state of New York
explicit_text: In Manila, 1941, our CO was Boyd "Buzz" Wagner. He was a *West Point* grad, one of the first WWII aces. Later, after a dog fight, Buzz was blinded but managed to land. I remember thinking how tough those *West Point* officers were.
implicit_text: In Manila, 1941, our CO was Boyd "Buzz" Wagner. He'd come from that officer training school, and was one of the first WWII aces. Later, after a dog fight, Buzz was blinded but managed to land. I remember thinking how tough those officers were, the ones who'd gone there.

entity_description: land service branch of the United States Armed Forces
explicit_text: In 1941, I was drafted into the United States Army. After basic at Fort Ord, I was sent to Letterman General for medical training. I even played baseball for them. After Pearl Harbor, things changed drastically.
implicit_text: In 1941, I was drafted. After basic training at Fort Ord, I was sent to Letterman General for medical training. I even played baseball for them. After Pearl Harbor, things changed drastically.

entity_description: public research university in West Lafayette, Indiana, United States
explicit_text: During my service, I sought every training opportunity. I remember taking a personnel administration course at Purdue University. It was quite something, because the president actually quit while I was there! This was just one stop in my journey, happening sometime after my time at Fort Des Moines.
implicit_text: During my service, I sought every training opportunity. I remember taking a personnel administration course at a university in West Lafayette. entity_description: public university in Williamsburg, Virginia
explicit_text: During my service, I kept seeking further training. Funny thing, at Purdue, then later at William & Mary in Virginia, each time I attended, the university president resigned! After that, the same thing happened at Washington and Lee. It was quite a coincidence.
implicit_text: During my service, I kept seeking further training. Funny thing, at Purdue, then later at that historic institution in Williamsburg, Virginia, each time I attended, the university president resigned! After that, the same thing happened at Washington and Lee. It was quite a coincidence.

entity_description: private liberal arts university in Lexington, Virginia
explicit_text: After Purdue and William & Mary, I was sent to **Washington and Lee University** to train as a special service officer. It seemed like every time I went to a school, the president quit! Being the only black trainee in those facilities back then, around 1940s, was something else.

implicit_text: They shipped me to France. I joined the 103rd medical battalion, attached to the 28th division. It was '44, and I was fresh out of training. Volunteered because I was young and single, right into the thick of it.

entity_description: public research university in London, United Kingdom; constituent college of the federal University of London

implicit_text: I shipped out in '43, headed for England. We traveled to England on that famous ship. It was toward the end of '43, October I think, after training stateside. Quite a journey on that vessel.

entity_description: American icon in New York, United States

explicit_text: After training, I was sent across the country by train. We arrived in San Francisco, preparing to ship out. But then, Pearl Harbor was bombed. We were diverted to Schofield Barracks, unsure of what was next.

entity_description: Vietnam People's Air Force base

entity_description: largest city in Vietnam

entity_description: island in the Mediterranean, region of Italy
explicit_text: In '43, we landed in Casablanca, then Tunisia. Next, we invaded Sicily. I even met family there; my grandpa proudly showed me off in my uniform near Palermo. After Sicily, it was on to Naples-Foggia.
implicit_text: In '43, we landed in Casablanca, then Tunisia. Next, we invaded *that* island. I even met family there; my grandpa proudly showed me off in my uniform near Palermo. After *that*, it was on to Naples-Foggia.

entity_description: capital and largest city of Italy
explicit_text: In '43, we landed in Casablanca and fought through Sicily and Naples. We invaded all the way up to Rome. When we reached Rome, some of us even got a blessing from the Pope at the Vatican. It was quite a journey.
implicit_text: In '43, we landed in Casablanca and fought through Sicily and Naples. We invaded all the way up the Italian peninsula. When we reached the capital, some of us even got a blessing from the Pope at the Vatican. It was quite a journey.

entity_description: Holy See's independent state, an enclave within Rome, Italy

implicit_text: After fighting through Italy in '44, we reached Rome. A group of us soldiers went to the small state within the city. We received a blessing from the Pope. It was a powerful moment.

entity_description: capital of and state in Austria
explicit_text: The boxcars were rough, then a bombed-out train. We stopped in Amherst, Germany, and some French towns. Finally, we arrived. Our destination was Vienna, Austria.
implicit_text: The boxcars were rough, then a bombed-out train. We stopped in Amherst, Germany, and some French towns. Finally, we arrived. Our destination was the Austrian capital.

entity_description: palace in Vienna, Austria

We didn't have time for sightseeing, though.

entity_description: capital city of the United States of America

explicit_text: We were in Germany, early May '45. The war was grinding down, you could feel it. Then, boom, we had just declared VE Day. Celebrations erupted, but we knew the Pacific was still raging. It was a strange mix of relief and knowing there was still work to be done.

explicit_text: Growing up near Boston, Concord was literary hallowed ground. We revered Hawthorne, Thoreau, and Ralph Waldo Emerson. Emerson, especially, embodied that rural, rustic ideal. His essays shaped our view of the world, even as we grappled with the social issues of the time.

entity_description: American sociologist and activist (1868–1963)
explicit_text: At Harvard in '22, Donald Peattie invited me to hear W. E. B. Du Bois at the Liberal Club. I initially refused, saying I couldn't dine with a "Negro." My roommates argued until I relented. Du Bois made a great speech; it was my first break from Southern tradition.

entity_description: daily broadsheet newspaper in Washington, D.C.

entity_description: city in southeastern Maryland, United States

entity_description: African-American civil rights organization
explicit_text: Before the Albany Movement really took off in '61, we knew Dr. King and the Southern Christian Leadership Conference were getting involved. entity_description: upper house of the United States Congress
